title: O(n) LFU eviction algorithm
description: LFU eviction iterates through all cache entries to find the entry with minimum hits. For a cache with n entries, each eviction requires O(n) time, and batch evictions become O(n²).
fix: Use a min-heap (heapq) keyed by hit count, or maintain a sorted structure like sortedcontainers.SortedDict to enable O(log n) eviction.

title: Unbounded histogram and timing lists in InMemoryMetricHook
description: InMemoryMetricHook stores histograms and timings as unbounded lists: `self.histograms: dict[str, list[float]]` and `self.timings: dict[str, list[float]]`. In long-running processes, these lists grow without limit, causing memory exhaustion.
fix: Use collections.deque with maxlen to bound the number of samples, or periodically aggregate/summarize old data and discard raw samples.

title: Unbounded _events list in InMemoryAuditLogger
description: InMemoryAuditLogger stores all audit events in `self._events: list[AuditEventV2]` without any size limit. In production systems with high audit volume, this will cause unbounded memory growth.
fix: Add a max_events parameter and implement FIFO eviction when the limit is reached, or use deque(maxlen=N).

title: Unbounded _session_records dictionary
description: SessionCostTracker stores usage records per session in `self._session_records: dict[str, list[UsageRecord]]` without cleanup. Completed or expired sessions are never removed, causing memory to grow indefinitely.
fix: Implement session expiration with periodic cleanup, or use a TTL-based cache like cachetools.TTLCache for session records.

title: O(n²) peak rate calculation algorithm
description: The peak rate calculation uses nested loops iterating over all records: `for i in range(len(records) - 1): for j in range(i, len(records)):`. For sessions with many usage records, this becomes O(n²) which can cause significant latency.
fix: Use a sliding window algorithm with two pointers, or maintain a running sum as the window slides. This reduces complexity to O(n).

title: O(n²) message limit enforcement
description: MessageHistory._enforce_limits() uses nested loops: the outer while loop runs up to (messages - max_messages) times, and each iteration scans the list to find a non-system message. Worst case is O(n²) when many messages need removal.
fix: Partition messages into system and non-system lists, then truncate the non-system list directly. Alternatively, use a deque for non-system messages.

title: O(n²) session limit enforcement in SessionManager
description: SessionManager._enforce_limits() has the same pattern as MessageHistory: nested loops that repeatedly scan the list to find removable items. This causes O(n²) behavior when enforcing limits on large session lists.
fix: Track sessions in separate data structures by type, or use indexed collections that allow O(1) removal of the oldest non-protected session.

title: Unbounded user spend tracking dictionaries
description: CostTracker maintains `_user_daily_spend` and `_user_hourly_spend` as nested dicts that grow without cleanup. Old date keys are never removed, causing memory growth proportional to (users × days) or (users × hours).
fix: Implement periodic cleanup of old date/hour keys, or use a TTL-based structure. Consider keeping only the current day/hour and previous period for comparison.

title: Potential ReDoS in complex regex patterns
description: Several compiled regex patterns use nested quantifiers and alternations that could cause catastrophic backtracking on malicious input. Patterns like those detecting prompt injection may be vulnerable to specially crafted strings.
fix: Audit each pattern for ReDoS vulnerability using tools like safe-regex or regex-static-analysis. Consider using possessive quantifiers, atomic groups, or refactoring to avoid nested quantifiers.

title: Potential ReDoS in data leakage detection patterns
description: OutputGuard regex patterns for detecting sensitive data (SSNs, credit cards, API keys) use multiple alternations and quantifiers. While less likely to be exploited than input patterns, they still warrant review.
fix: Review patterns for catastrophic backtracking. Consider using non-backtracking engines (like re2) for security-critical pattern matching, or add input length limits before regex matching.

title: Large maxlen for execution_history deque
description: ProxilionVertexHandler uses `deque(maxlen=10000)` for execution_history. While bounded, storing 10,000 execution records per handler instance can consume significant memory in systems with many concurrent handlers.
fix: Consider reducing maxlen based on actual usage patterns, or implementing a sampling strategy for long-running sessions.

title: Synchronous file I/O in audit logging hot path
description: AuditLogger.log() performs synchronous file writes with JSON serialization on every audit event. In high-throughput scenarios, this blocks the calling thread and can become a bottleneck.
fix: Implement asynchronous logging with a background writer thread and bounded queue, or batch writes at configurable intervals.
